questions.question: What are the main steps of an end-to-end machine learning project?
options: Look at big picture, get data, explore, prepare, select model, fine-tune, present, launch
questions.explanation: The main steps are: 1) Look at the big picture, 2) Get the data, 3) Explore and visualize the data, 4) Prepare the data, 5) Select and train a model, 6) Fine-tune the model, 7) Present the solution, 8) Launch, monitor, and maintain the system.

questions.question: What is a data pipeline?
options: A sequence of data processing components
questions.explanation: A data pipeline is a sequence of data processing components. Pipelines are very common in ML systems since there is a lot of data to manipulate and many data transformations to apply.

questions.question: Why is it important to know the business objective before building a model?
options: It determines problem framing, algorithm selection, and performance measure
questions.explanation: Knowing the business objective is important because it determines how you frame the problem, which algorithms you select, which performance measure you use, and how much effort you spend tweaking the model.

questions.question: In the California housing example, what type of machine learning task is it?
questions.explanation: The California housing problem is a supervised regression task (specifically multiple univariate regression) since the model predicts a continuous value (median housing price) using multiple features.

questions.question: What is RMSE?
options: Root Mean Square Error - a typical performance measure for regression
questions.explanation: RMSE (Root Mean Square Error) is a typical performance measure for regression problems. It gives an idea of how much error the system typically makes in its predictions, with higher weight given to large errors.

questions.question: When might you prefer MAE over RMSE?
options: When you want to penalize large errors more
When there are many outlier districts
questions.explanation: You might prefer MAE (Mean Absolute Error) over RMSE when there are many outlier districts, because RMSE penalizes large errors more heavily than MAE.

questions.question: What is stratified sampling?
options: Random sampling from the entire population
Sampling only from the largest groups
Dividing population into homogeneous subgroups and sampling from each
questions.explanation: Stratified sampling divides the population into homogeneous subgroups called strata, and the right number of instances are sampled from each stratum to guarantee that the test set is representative of the overall population.

questions.question: What is data snooping bias?
options: Bias from looking at test set before model selection
questions.explanation: Data snooping bias occurs when you look at the test set before selecting your model. You may stumble upon patterns that lead you to select a particular model, making your generalization error estimate too optimistic.

questions.question: What are the three options for handling missing values in a dataset?
options: Get rid of districts, get rid of attribute, or set to some value (imputation)
Delete all data, ignore it, or guess
Fill with zeros, ones, or random values
Ask users, skip model, or use default
questions.explanation: The three options for handling missing values are: 1) Get rid of the corresponding districts, 2) Get rid of the whole attribute, 3) Set the missing values to some value (zero, mean, median, etc.) - this is called imputation.

questions.question: What is the SimpleImputer in Scikit-Learn used for?
options: Replacing missing values with median, mean, or most frequent value
questions.explanation: SimpleImputer is used to replace missing values. It can use different strategies like median, mean, most_frequent, or constant values. It stores the learned values to apply the same imputation to new data.

questions.question: What is the difference between OrdinalEncoder and OneHotEncoder?
options: OrdinalEncoder assigns integers; OneHotEncoder creates binary columns
questions.explanation: OrdinalEncoder converts categorical text values to integers. OneHotEncoder creates binary columns (one per category), which is better for ML algorithms that assume numerical values imply ordering.

questions.question: Why might OneHotEncoder be preferred over pandas get_dummies()?
options: It remembers training categories and handles unknown categories consistently
questions.explanation: OneHotEncoder remembers which categories it was trained on, ensuring the same features are used in production. It handles unknown categories properly, while get_dummies() may produce different columns for different inputs.

questions.question: What is an estimator in Scikit-Learn?
options: Any object that can estimate parameters based on a dataset
questions.explanation: An estimator is any object that can estimate some parameters based on a dataset. The estimation is performed by the fit() method. Examples include SimpleImputer and machine learning models.

questions.question: What is a transformer in Scikit-Learn?
options: An estimator that can transform a dataset using learned parameters
questions.explanation: A transformer is an estimator that can transform a dataset. The transformation is performed by the transform() method. Transformers also have fit_transform() which is equivalent to calling fit() then transform().

questions.question: What is the purpose of fit_transform() method?
options: Equivalent to calling fit() then transform(), sometimes optimized
questions.explanation: fit_transform() is equivalent to calling fit() and then transform(), but sometimes it is optimized and runs much faster than calling both methods separately.

questions.question: What is a predictor in Scikit-Learn?
options: An estimator capable of making predictions with predict() and score() methods
questions.explanation: A predictor is an estimator capable of making predictions. It has a predict() method that takes new instances and returns predictions, and a score() method to measure prediction quality.

questions.question: What is feature scaling and why is it important?
options: Making features have similar scales so algorithms converge faster
questions.explanation: Feature scaling ensures that features have similar scales. This is important because many ML algorithms (like gradient descent) converge faster when features are on similar scales.

questions.question: What is the difference between min-max scaling and standardization?
options: Min-max scales to 0-1 range; standardization centers at 0 with unit variance
questions.explanation: Min-max scaling transforms features to a 0-1 range. Standardization subtracts the mean and divides by standard deviation, resulting in values centered at 0 with unit variance.

questions.question: When is it best to create a test set before exploring the data?
options: Immediately, before exploring data to avoid data snooping bias
questions.explanation: You should create a test set immediately and put it aside before exploring the data. This prevents data snooping bias where you might select a model based on patterns seen in the test data.

questions.question: What is the typical test set size ratio?
questions.explanation: Typically 20% of the dataset is set aside as the test set. If the dataset is very large, you might use less than 20%.

questions.question: What is multiple regression?
options: Using multiple features to make a prediction
questions.explanation: Multiple regression means the system uses multiple features to make a prediction. In the housing example, features like population, median income, etc. are used together.

questions.question: What is univariate regression?
options: Predicting a single value for each instance
questions.explanation: Univariate regression means predicting a single value for each instance. Multivariate regression would predict multiple values per instance.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using hash-based splitting for train/test sets?
options: The test set remains consistent across multiple runs even with updated data
questions.explanation: Hash-based splitting using instance identifiers ensures the test set remains consistent across multiple runs, even when you refresh the dataset with new data.

questions.question: What does the random_state parameter in train_test_split() do?
options: Sets the random generator seed for reproducible results
questions.explanation: The random_state parameter allows you to set the random generator seed, ensuring that the same train/test split is generated every time you run the code.

questions.question: What is the benefit of writing functions for data preparation?
options: Allows reproducibility on any dataset, builds reusable library, works in live systems
questions.explanation: Writing functions for data preparation allows you to reproduce transformations on any dataset, build a library of reusable transformations, use them in live systems, and easily try various combinations.

questions.question: What is KNNImputer?
options: An imputer that replaces missing values using k-nearest neighbors
questions.explanation: KNNImputer replaces each missing value with the mean of the k-nearest neighbors' values for that feature, based on all available features.

questions.question: What is IterativeImputer?
options: An imputer that trains regression models to predict missing values iteratively
questions.explanation: IterativeImputer trains a regression model per feature to predict missing values based on all other available features, then iteratively improves the predictions.

questions.question: Why should you separate predictors and labels before transformation?
options: Because you don't necessarily want the same transformations on both
questions.explanation: You should separate predictors and labels because you don't necessarily want to apply the same transformations to the predictors and the target values.

questions.question: What is the main design principle of Scikit-Learn's API?
options: Consistency - all objects share a simple interface
questions.explanation: Scikit-Learn's main design principle is consistency. All estimators share a consistent and simple interface with fit(), transform(), and predict() methods.

questions.question: How are hyperparameters accessed in Scikit-Learn estimators?
questions.explanation: All estimator hyperparameters are accessible directly via public instance variables (e.g., imputer.strategy). Learned parameters are accessible via public instance variables with underscore suffix (e.g., imputer.statistics_).

questions.question: What are the popular open data repositories mentioned?
options: OpenML.org, Kaggle.com, PapersWithCode.com, UCI ML Repository
questions.explanation: Popular open data repositories include OpenML.org, Kaggle.com, PapersWithCode.com, UC Irvine Machine Learning Repository, AWS datasets, and TensorFlow datasets.

questions.question: What dataset is used in Chapter 2's example project?
questions.explanation: The California Housing Prices dataset from the StatLib repository is used. It's based on 1990 California census data with metrics like population, median income, and median housing price for each block group.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the housing pipeline?
options: To transform input data through a sequence of processing steps
To collect data from multiple sources
questions.explanation: A housing pipeline (or any ML pipeline) transforms input data through a sequence of processing steps. Each component pulls data, processes it, and outputs results for the next component.

questions.question: What makes a pipeline architecture robust?
options: Components are self-contained; downstream can continue if upstream breaks temporarily
questions.explanation: Pipeline architecture is robust because components are fairly self-contained. If a component breaks, downstream components can often continue running by using the last output from the broken component.

questions.question: What is a potential problem with pipeline components?
options: A broken component may go unnoticed without proper monitoring, causing stale data
questions.explanation: A broken component can go unnoticed without proper monitoring. The data gets stale and the overall system's performance drops.

questions.question: What is batch learning suitable for?
options: Very large datasets that don't fit in memory
Static datasets small enough to fit in memory
questions.explanation: Batch learning is suitable when there is no continuous flow of data, no need to rapidly adjust to changing data, and the data is small enough to fit in memory.

questions.question: What should you do if your dataset is huge for batch learning?
options: Use MapReduce across multiple servers or use online learning
questions.explanation: If the data is huge for batch learning, you can either split your batch learning work across multiple servers using MapReduce techniques or use an online learning technique.

questions.question: What is the notation 'm' in RMSE formula?
options: The number of instances in the dataset
questions.explanation: In the RMSE formula, 'm' represents the number of instances in the dataset you are measuring the RMSE on.

questions.question: What does the notation X (uppercase bold) represent?
options: A matrix containing all feature values of all instances
questions.explanation: X (uppercase bold) is a matrix containing all feature values (excluding labels) of all instances. There is one row per instance.

questions.question: What does the notation x (lowercase bold) represent?
options: A vector of feature values for a single instance
questions.explanation: x (lowercase bold) is a vector of all feature values (excluding the label) of a single instance in the dataset.

questions.question: What does y-hat (ŷ) represent?
options: The predicted value for an instance
questions.explanation: ŷ (y-hat) represents the predicted value for an instance, computed by the hypothesis function h(x). The prediction error is ŷ - y.

questions.question: What is the risk of purely random sampling?
options: It can introduce sampling bias if the dataset is not large enough
It always produces the same split
questions.explanation: Purely random sampling is fine for large datasets but can introduce significant sampling bias if the dataset is not large enough relative to the number of attributes.

questions.question: Why was the income category attribute created for stratified sampling?
options: To ensure the test set is representative of various income categories
questions.explanation: The income category attribute was created because median income is a very important attribute to predict housing prices. Stratified sampling ensures the test set represents various income categories.

questions.question: How many income categories were created in the example?
questions.explanation: Five income categories were created using pd.cut(): category 1 (0-1.5), category 2 (1.5-3.0), category 3 (3.0-4.5), category 4 (4.5-6.0), and category 5 (6.0+).

questions.question: What is the purpose of the StratifiedShuffleSplit class?
options: To generate multiple stratified splits of the same dataset
To split data into equal parts
questions.explanation: StratifiedShuffleSplit generates multiple stratified splits of the same dataset. This is useful for cross-validation to better estimate model performance.

questions.question: What should you do with the temporary income_cat column after stratified sampling?
options: Drop it to revert data to original state
Use it as the target variable
questions.explanation: After stratified sampling, you should drop the income_cat column to revert the data back to its original state since it won't be used again.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the dropna() method?
options: To remove rows with missing values
questions.explanation: The dropna() method removes rows with missing values. You can specify a subset of columns to check for missing values.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the fillna() method?
options: To fill missing values with a specified value
questions.explanation: The fillna() method fills missing values with a specified value. Common choices include the median, mean, or a constant value.

questions.question: Why create a copy of data with only numerical attributes before using SimpleImputer?
options: Because median can only be computed on numerical attributes
Because SimpleImputer doesn't work with text
questions.explanation: Since the median can only be computed on numerical attributes, you need to create a copy of the data with only the numerical attributes (excluding text attributes like ocean_proximity).

questions.question: What does the statistics_ instance variable of SimpleImputer contain?
options: The computed median (or other strategy value) of each attribute
questions.explanation: The statistics_ instance variable contains the computed values (e.g., median) for each attribute that were learned during fit(). These are used to transform new data.
